mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 320d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 320d engine typically costs £2,800-£5,800 supply only depending on generation, with the current B47D20 sitting at £3,400-£5,800. Fitting adds £650-£1,300. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 320d N47 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on which of the four engine generations your 320d has - this badge spans the M47 (1998-2005, excellent reliability, no chain risk), the N47 (2005-2015/16, the well-documented rear-chain risk generation), and the B47 (2015/16-present, chain relocated to the front, meaningfully improved).

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 320d?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain wear on N47-engined cars, swirl flap failure on the M47 150bhp variant and early N47s, and an EGR cooler coolant leak affecting both N47 and B47 F30-era cars, subject to a BMW recall.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 320d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept £7,000 320d, a £4,500 recon is 64% of the car's value - worth it. Against a tired £3,500 example, the same recon exceeds our 70% BMW threshold - get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 320d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: pre-2005 is M47, 2005-2015/16 is N47, 2015/16-onward is B47. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N47 timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, if caught early. A preventative chain replacement (£650-£1,800) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain snap (£3,200-£5,500 engine replacement). At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately.

eraMap.title: The 320d Through Time — Era Map

rows.eraNote: Widely regarded as one of BMW's most durable diesels — no rear-chain risk. Swirl flaps (150bhp variant, 2001+) are the one real weak point.

rows.reliability: ★★★☆☆ 1,450 JLR-wide [BMW-VERIFIED — inherited from Model Page & E90 Generation Page]
rows.eraNote: The defining rear-mounted timing-chain generation. See the full E90 Generation Page for the M57/N57 diesel-six story alongside this engine.

rows.reliability: ★★★☆☆ pre-facelift / ★★★★☆ post-facelift [NEWLY ASSIGNED — see Data Note]
rows.eraNote: BMW's 2011 chain revision improved on the E90 N47 but didn't eliminate the rear-chain risk. The 2015/16 facelift replaced it entirely with the B47 — chain relocated to the front, a genuinely different risk profile.
The modern, most-reliable generation of this badge — still a chain-tensioned engine, so not risk-free, but the furthest removed from the rear-chain story that defines this badge's reputation.

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.
eraMap.dataNote: E90's N47D20C rating and its 1,450 JLR-wide enquiry figure are real, already-published facts inherited from the BMW 3 Series Model Page and the validated E90 Generation Page — not re-derived here. M47 (E46) and B47 (F30 post-facelift, G20) have no dedicated Engine Page yet, so their ratings are newly assigned on this page, based on live verification: M47's ★★★★½ reflects a strong, consistent cross-source consensus (E46Fanatics, Euro Racing Parts buying guide, TorqueCars, Wikipedia, RMS Motoring, Baby BMW forum, engineexplained.com — 7 sources, all converging on "bulletproof"/"rock-solid"/exceeds 300,000km, with swirl flaps as the only material weak point on the 150bhp variant). B47's ★★★★☆ reflects Wikipedia (BMW B47, BMW 3 Series F30) plus a dedicated BMW-specialist reliability guide confirming the chain relocation to the front eliminated the specific rear-chain failure mode, while noting the B47 still requires timing-chain monitoring generally. Both should be treated as provisional pending dedicated M47 and B47 Engine Pages.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current G20/late-F30 B47D20 generation, the most common quote request today. Older generations: see the Era Map above for E46/M47 and E90/N47-specific pricing, which runs lower.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 320d engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

problems.title: N47 Rear Timing Chain Failure (E90, early F30)
problems.symptoms: Cold-start rattle lasting 1–30 seconds, metallic ticking, engine warning light.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: This is the single most searched 320d issue for good reason — at the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the chain inspected. A £650–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,200+ replacement that follows.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide →

problems.title: Swirl Flap Failure (M47 150bhp variant, E46/early E90)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, rough idle, engine warning light, occasional rattling from the intake.
problems.replacementCost: £850–£1,200 (intake manifold, or engine repair if debris entered a cylinder).
problems.recommendation: This affects the M47's 150bhp common-rail variant and remains a known issue on early N47s too. Preventive blanking is cheap, effective, and removes the risk entirely with no noticeable performance loss.

failureLink.label: Read the full swirl flap failure guide →

problems.title: EGR Cooler Coolant Leak (N47 and B47, all F30-era)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, white exhaust smoke, occasional burning smell.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — this was the subject of a global BMW recall affecting both N47 and B47 diesel variants.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — this doesn't typically progress to engine replacement if addressed, but ignored coolant loss can lead to overheating damage.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: check recall completion status on any F30-era 320d.

problems.recommendation: Verify recall completion before buying any N47 or B47-engined F30 320d — this is a straightforward, well-documented fix when caught early.

failureLink.label: Read the full EGR cooler failure guide →

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches the fault before it becomes catastrophic
Blanking plates remove the risk entirely
Straightforward fix if caught before overheating
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair
Viable on lower-mileage examples; a lottery above 250,000km

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 320d? Check These First
buyIf: Documented timing-chain history if buying an E90 or pre-2016 F30
Confirmed EGR recall completion on any F30-era car
Full service history with sub-15,000km oil intervals
A post-2016 B47 if you want the cleanest slate of any 320d generation
avoidIf: E90 or pre-2016 F30 with cold-start rattle and no chain history
No proof of swirl flap blanking or replacement on an M47 150bhp or early N47
Poor service history on any generation — this badge is unforgiving of neglect
Engine warning light with no diagnosis, regardless of generation

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: Across four decades of this badge, the honest advice barely changes: buy on documented history, not on generation alone. The M47 and post-2016 B47 are this badge's two genuinely low-risk eras; the E90 and early F30 N47 demand real chain-history proof before you commit.

cta.label: Compare 320d engine prices before you buy →

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 320d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: Cold Start — Listen for 1–30 seconds of rattle (timing chain warning, N47/early F30)
Service History — including which engine era (M47/N47/B47) and confirmed oil-interval discipline
Smoke Check — White smoke on startup can indicate EGR coolant leak
Turbo Boost — Test acceleration; any lag, whistling, or grinding?
DPF Regeneration — Check history; short journeys accelerate wear on all diesel eras
Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.
